heb.paragraph: "Blessed is the one whose transgressions are forgiven (nəsûy feshaʿ), whose sins are covered" (v.1). The second penitential psalm. Three words for sin: transgression (rebellion), sin (missing the mark), iniquity (crookedness). Three words for forgiveness: forgiven (carried away), covered (concealed), not counted (not reckoned). Paul quotes vv.1–2 in Romans 4:7–8 for justification by faith.

"Then I acknowledged (yādîṭî) my sin to you and did not cover up my iniquity" (v.5). The turning point: confession. While David was silent, his bones wasted (vv.3–4). When he confessed, God forgave. The contrast: concealment produces decay; confession produces freedom.

refs.note: Paul quotes Psalm 32:1–2 as proof of justification by faith apart from works: "Blessed are those whose transgressions are forgiven."
"If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just and will forgive us" — the NT’s version of David’s experience.
The companion penitential psalm, more explicitly tied to David’s adultery with Bathsheba.

notes.note: "Blessed whose transgressions are forgiven, whose sins are covered. Blessed the one whose sin the LORD does not count against them." Three sins, three removals. Comprehensive forgiveness.

"When I kept silent, my bones wasted away through my groaning all day long. Day and night your hand was heavy on me." Unconfessed sin produces physical deterioration. The body carries what the mouth refuses to speak.
"Then I acknowledged my sin to you. I said, ‘I will confess.’ And you forgave the guilt of my sin." The confession is instantaneous; so is the forgiveness. No penance, no delay.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: three words for sin paired with three words for removal. The catalogue of sin is matched by a catalogue of grace. Nothing is left uncovered. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.
Calvin: Paul’s use of this text in Romans 4 makes it foundational for justification. Calvin: forgiveness is not moral improvement but legal declaration: sin is not counted.
Calvin: the speed of forgiveness shatters every notion of earned pardon. David confessed; God forgave. The interval between confession and forgiveness is zero.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: three sin terms: peshaʿ (rebellion/transgression), ḥaṭṭāʾâ (sin/missing), ʿāwōn (iniquity/crookedness). Three forgiveness terms: nāsûy (lifted/carried away), kəsûy (covered), lōʾ yaḥshōb (not reckoned).
The NET: the instant forgiveness — "you forgave the guilt of my sin" — uses the verb nāsāʾ (to lift/carry away). God removes sin as completely as lifting a load off someone’s back.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the physical symptoms of unconfessed sin — wasting bones, groaning, drained strength — are not divine punishment but the natural consequence of internal dishonesty. The body revolts against the soul’s lie. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: this is the psalm Paul uses for imputed righteousness (Rom 4:6–8). Forgiveness is not earned by confession; it is declared by God. Confession is the occasion, not the cause. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the three-stage movement (silence → confession → forgiveness) takes place in one verse. The breakthrough is instantaneous. The suffering was in the delay, not in the process. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: David describes the misery of unconfessed sin: groaning, bones wasting, strength sapped (vv.3–4). Then the liberation of confession: "I said, ‘I will confess,’ and you forgave the guilt of my sin" (v.5). God is a hiding place, a protector, surrounded by songs of deliverance (v.7). The emotional arc: guilt → silence → decay → confession → forgiveness → joy.

sections.header: Verses 8–11 — “I Will Instruct You and Teach You”

heb.paragraph: The LORD's unfailing love surrounds (סבב - sabab) the one who trusts in him

refs.note: "Trust in the LORD... he will make your paths straight" — the same guidance theology.
"Confess your sins to each other and pray for each other so that you may be healed" — the community application.

notes.note: "I will instruct you and teach you in the way you should go." God becomes personal tutor. The eye of God guides, not just observes.
"Do not be like the horse or the mule." Animals require external control because they lack understanding. God wants willing followers, not controlled beasts.

"Many are the woes of the wicked, but the LORD’s unfailing love surrounds the one who trusts. Rejoice!" The psalm ends in celebration: trust produces surrounded love, and surrounded love produces joy.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the horse-and-mule image is earthy and comic. God compares the stubborn person to a pack animal requiring hardware. The sarcasm is loving. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.
Calvin: the contrast is binary: woes vs love, wicked vs trusting. Calvin: there is no third category. You are either inside the ḥesed-perimeter or outside in woe.
Calvin: the command to rejoice follows naturally from forgiveness. Calvin: joylessness in the Christian life is often unconfessed sin blocking the experience of declared pardon.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "with my eye on you" (ʿēynî) can also be translated "I will counsel you, my eye upon you" — God watches over the guided person like a parent watching a child learn to walk.
The NET: sāmak̅ (surrounds/encompasses) is the same verb used for an army surrounding a city. Ḥesed besieges the trusting person with love.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the horse and mule proverb is God’s gentle humour. The bit and bridle are for animals; God prefers to guide by eye contact. Choose the higher relationship. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the psalm moves from confession (vv.1–5) to instruction (vv.8–9) to celebration (vv.10–11). The forgiven person becomes the taught person, then the joyful person. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "surrounds" is comprehensive. Not love beside you or ahead of you but encircling you. The trusting person is inside a perimeter of ḥesed. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: God speaks: "I will instruct you and teach you in the way you should go; I will counsel you with my loving eye on you" (v.8). The warning: "Do not be like the horse or the mule, which have no understanding but must be controlled by bit and bridle" (v.9). The contrast (v.10): woes for the wicked, surrounding love for the trusting. The psalm closes: "Rejoice in the LORD and be glad, you righteous; sing, all you who are upright in heart!" (v.11).

I said, “I will confess my transgressions to the Lord.” And you forgave the guilt of my sin.ESVI acknowledged my sin to you, and I did not cover my iniquity; I said, \u201cI will confess my transgressions to the LORD,\u201d and you forgave the iniquity of my sin. Selah

rec.text: ""Blessed whose transgressions are forgiven, whose sins are covered. Blessed the one whose sin the LORD does not count against them." Three sins, three removals. Comprehensive forgiveness."

rows.label: Guided by God’s Eye — 32:8–11
rows.text: Not horse or mule. "Love surrounds the one who trusts." Rejoice!

hebtext.note: "Blessed is the one whose transgressions are forgiven (nəsûy feshaʿ), whose sins are covered" (v.1). The second penitential psalm. Three words for sin: transgression (rebellion), sin (missing the mark), iniquity (crookedness). Three words for forgiveness: forgiven (carried away), covered (concealed), not counted (not reckoned). Paul quotes vv.1–2 in Romans 4:7–8 for justification by faith.

"Then I acknowledged (yādîṭî) my sin to you and did not cover up my iniquity" (v.5). The turning point: confession. While David was silent, his bones wasted (vv.3–4). When he confessed, God forgave. The contrast: concealment produces decay; confession produces freedom.
The LORD's unfailing love surrounds (סָבַב - sabab) the one who trusts in him (v.10). The Hebrew root is sabab meaning 'to surround/encompass,' not samakh.

thread.text: Paul quotes Psalm 32:1–2 as proof of justification by faith apart from works: "Blessed are those whose transgressions are forgiven."
"If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just and will forgive us" — the NT’s version of David’s experience.
The companion penitential psalm, more explicitly tied to David’s adultery with Bathsheba.
"Trust in the LORD... he will make your paths straight" — the same guidance theology.
"Confess your sins to each other and pray for each other so that you may be healed" — the community application.

themes.note: Mercy peaks: instant forgiveness, surrounding love. Prophecy: Paul’s justification text (Rom 4:7–8). Worship: the command to rejoice after confession.
